data.title: Iron Mountain: King Of Storage And Dispositions Offers Robust Return Prospects
data.description:
I previously covered Iron Mountain Incorporated (NYSE:IRM) in May 2025, discussing how the REIT's well diversified capabilities as a physical and digital storage provider had paid off handsomely, as the legacy Global RIM business delivered a rich cash flow business as they also capitalize on the ongoing data center boom.

Combined with the raised FY2025 guidance, the cheaper valuations, and the materialization of a much needed floor during the recent meltdown, I had believed its investment thesis remained compelling then.

In this article, I shall discuss how IRM's Matterhorn Strategy: Accelerating Growth has worked out brilliantly, with it triggering the accelerated Growth Businesses segment at a 4Y CAGR of +20% and the projected 2026 data center revenues of $1B (5Y CAGR of +25%).

Combined with the excellent server decommissioning prospects arising from the accelerated server refresh cycle, the rich total operational return prospect of 15.18%, and the last dividend raise by +9.7%, the REIT offers a unique way of tapping into the multi-year cloud super cycle, resulting in my reiterated Buy rating.

IRM Boasts A Well-Diversified Storage Ecosystem & Prudent Financing Method During The Ongoing Cloud Boom

IRM 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating after a moderate retracement to the prior support ranges of between the $82s and the $89s, my Buy Zone has materialized by mid August 2025 indeed, with IRM also rallying by +13.6% since then.

A similar development has been observed in data center focused REIT peers in varying degrees, attributed to the cloud segment's "tight demand supply environment" where the world's largest five hyperscalers by market share have already hinted at higher AI related spending trends in 2026.

A similar development has been observed in many smaller (yet extremely well funded) players as well, including neoclouds and even, bitcoin miners turned HPC providers, with it apparent that we remain in the early innings of the multi-year cloud super cycle.

With IRM set to report their earning performance in November 05, 2025, readers may want to compare their upcoming performance against:

  • the FQ3'25 revenue growth guidance of +12% YoY, adj EBITDA at +14% YoY, and AFFO per share at +13% YoY offered in the FQ2'25 earnings call, against
  • the twice raised FY2025 guidance at +11.5% YoY, +13.8% YoY, and +12% YoY, respectively.

When compared to the FY2024 outperformance at +12.2% YoY, +13.7% YoY, and +10.1% YoY, respectively, the storage REIT has indeed been extremely successful in its diversification across physical ecosystem supporting information storage and retrieval to a hybrid of physical/ digital media ecosystem.

These reasons are also why IRM's Matterhorn Strategy: Accelerating Growth has worked out brilliantly, since the aggressive investments in its digital businesses (including Data Center FQ2'25 revenue growth by +26% YoY and Asset Lifecycle Management [ALM] by +70% YoY) have triggered the accelerated Growth Businesses segment at a 4Y CAGR of +20%.

This is on top of the projected expansion of the Growth Businesses revenue share at 28% by 2025, up notably from the 15% reported in 2021, with the REIT's excellent ability to cross-sell its vertically integrated storage solutions to its growing customer base already triggering the similarly rich bottom-line growth profile over the past few years.

For example, IRM's aggressive data center investments have already bore fruit, with 2025 to deliver nearly +30% YoY revenue growth and a long-term capacity growth from 450MW to 1.3 GW, with it providing an excellent line of sight to 2026 data center revenues of $1B (expanding at a 5Y CAGR of +25%).

This is significantly aided by the higher renewal pricing by +13% YoY, with the tight supply environment triggering the REIT's improved pricing power and consequently, richer top/ bottom-line performance.

At the same time, IRM's ALM services have also gone beyond physical records to its data center offerings through the decommissioning, data erasure, processing and disposition, and recycling of the partner hyperscalers' server components across the memory/CPU/GPUs, with it already comprising 40% of its ALM revenues.

With an accelerated server refresh cycle of "1 to 2 years, or up to 3 years" and Nvidia (NVDA) (and other infrastructure providers) already launching new AI chips on a yearly basis, I believe that the accelerated replacement cycle is likely to drive the REIT's long-term growth prospects.

Combined with the lower churn rate and the higher utilization across its vertically integrated storage offerings, I believe that IRM presents a unique breed of investment class for those looking to tap into the multi-year cloud super cycle.

IRM Valuations

Despite the notable upgrade from the pre-2013 data center ambition levels of 9.5x and the sector median of 14.73x, I believe that IRM still looks very compelling at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 20.02x by the time of writing, thanks to the much needed correction from the October 2024 peak of 26.91x.

When comparing it to its data center REIT peers, including:

  • Digital Realty Trust (DLR) at FWD Price/ AFFO valuations of 25.71x/ 5Y mean of 22.30x,
  • Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) at 20.91x/ 24.16x, and
  • American Tower Corporation (AMT) at 17.68x/ 22.23x, respectively,

it is apparent that IRM remains reasonably valued at current levels.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This is especially since IRM continues to report a healthy debt leverage of 5.0x, compared to DLR at 5.1x, EQIX at 3.5x, and AMT at 5.1x, along with the minimal shareholder equity erosion at -0.2% YoY, compared to its peers at +5.4% YoY, +3% YoY, and +0.2% YoY, respectively.

The latter is important indeed, since it allows the physical/ data center REIT to offer an estimated richer FFO per share growth at +12.7% through FY2027, compared to DLR at +8.2%, EQIX at +14%, and AMT at 0% over the same time period.

These metrics are also why I believe that the IRM management has been executing brilliantly, with the prudent financing method across debt and (inherent lack of) dilutive capital raises already triggering the richer earnings per share growth and the relatively more attractive valuations.

IRM Remains A Great Buy After A Slight Pullback

IRM 5Y Stock Price

For now, IRM has been consistently charting higher lows since the April 2025 bottom, albeit unable to break out beyond the established 2025 resistance levels of the $105s, as we enter the upcoming Q3'25 earnings season.

Despite this, I believe that the recent underperformance has been a boon, since the stock continues to trade nearer to my fair value estimates of $101.80, based on the management's FY2025 AFFO per share guidance of $5.085 at the midpoint (+12% YoY) and the current FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 20.02x (near to its peer group mean of 21x).

Based on the consensus FY2027 FFO per share estimates of $4.50 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +12.7%) and the current FWD Price/FFO valuations of 29.97x (not too far from the peer group mean of 26.5x), there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +32.3% to my long-term price target of $134.80.

At the same time, IRM's last dividend raise of +9.7% to $0.785 per share has well balanced the lower forward dividend yield of 3.08% as well, despite the underperformance against the 4Y mean yields of 3.92% and the sector median of 4.93%.

These have contributed to the extremely rich total operational return prospect of 15.18%, based on the 3Y AFFO per share growth at +12.1% and the forward dividend yield of 3.08%, compared to DLR at 12.4%, EQIX at 9.84%, and AMT at 8.34%.

As a result, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the IRM stock, with a few caveats to be discussed below.

The Risks Entering Q3'25 Earning Season

IRM's Technical Indicators

For now, IRM's technical indicators already suggest an overbought momentum and RSI, with it uncertain if the elevated trading volumes may break out of the first resistance line to retest the prior 2024 heights.

Combined with the potential tariff risks embedded in the wider market's bottom-lines from Q3'25 onwards and the ongoing trade discussions, my reiterated Buy rating comes with the caveat that investors observe the development for a little longer and only add nearer to the 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages of $96s/ $98s for an improved margin of safety.

Despite the fair valuations, the well balanced legacy/ data center capabilities, and the excellent return prospects, some patience may be more prudent, given the potential downside of -5.7% from current levels in the short-term.

data.title: WaterBridge Infrastructure: Visible Earnings Growth
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on WaterBridge Infrastructure (NYSE:WBI). WBI offers infrastructure-like stability and visible, high-quality growth, backed by long-term contracts with investment-grade producers. It also has access to underutilized pore space through LandBridge, which positions it to capture demand for water management in the Permian Basin. With major projects like Kraken, Speedway, and Cotton Draw coming online in 2029, I have a positive outlook on the EBITDA growth over the foreseeable future.

Company overview

WBI is a water midstream operator with the largest produced water infrastructure network in the US, with a footprint concentrated in the Delaware Basin and some exposure to the Eagle Ford and Arkoma basins. WBI provides end-to-end water management solutions, including gathering, transporting, recycling, and handling produced water, for oil and gas [O&G] E&P customers under long-term contracts. As of August 31, 2025, per the S-1 filings, the WBI pro forma network included ~2,500 miles of pipelines and 197 produced water handling facilities, and also two energy waste management sites for non-hazardous waste.

Moat

I believe WBI’s most durable competitive edge is its partnership with LandBridge (this is WBI’s affiliate). This relationship gives WBI preferential access to the Permian Basin, which is an underutilized pore space for underground water disposal. For easier understanding, you can think of this as like holding exclusive rights to the only available landfill in a city that has waste growing faster than what its existing wasteland can hold. In the context of WBI, as O&G producers move into deeper, higher-water zones, the volume of produced water grows (or what is known as an increase in the water-to-oil ratio), and that creates both logistical and regulatory bottlenecks. To give you a sense of how urgent the situation is, per management estimates in the S-1 filings, without new infrastructure, they expect the Delaware Basin to face the risk of limited water-handling capacity by 2029, which is just 4 years away.

Building water infrastructure itself is already not a simple task; compound that with the stricter permitting environment, especially in New Mexico, where approvals for new disposal wells have become slow and uncertain, and the entire situation becomes even worse. And this is why the access to LandBridge is so important. Not only because it gives WBI the extra capacity, but also because it is located in business-friendly Texas and low-pressure geological zones, which gives customers the ease of comfort knowing there is capacity reliability. Without this, E&P customers will be worried, as a lack of disposal logistics would mean a stoppage in drilling programs, which is costly.

All of this dynamic also means that WBI has strong pricing power. And we already have proof of this playing out in recent project economics, where new contracts are being signed at higher rates. I don’t foresee this dynamic going away anytime soon, as it is just very difficult to build another competing asset within a short timeframe. Moreover, attempting to do so would require not only massive capital investment but also aggregating land with the right surface rights and geological conditions.

Demand backed by contracts

Aside from WBI’s moat, I believe the key anchor for the equity story is its visible growth, one that is supported by project backlog. WBI now has three large-scale developments ongoing—Kraken, Speedway, and Cotton Draw—that are going to fully come online by 2027. These projects are backed by long-term take-or-pay agreements and meaningful minimum volume commitments [MVCs] with investment-grade counterparties such as BPX Energy and Devon Energy. Barring major economic recession or natural disasters, this contractual agreement basically “locks in” WBI’s growth.

The unit economics are also very compelling as well. Per the disclosures, these projects are being built at highly attractive, low-single-digit build multiples and contracted at rates meaningfully above WBI’s existing portfolio average. This links back to my point above about WBI’s pricing power, and as these high-margin assets come online between now and 2027, I expect them to meaningfully expand operating margins and accelerate earnings growth.

On the existing asset base, it is attractive too. The current contracts are also supported by long-term, fixed-fee agreements with an average remaining life of ~11 years. Most (~70%) of these contracts have a tenure of 15 years or more and include annual inflation-linked escalators. On top of that, when you consider that WBI customers include some of the most active producers in the Permian Basin, such as BPX Energy (a BP subsidiary), Chevron, Devon Energy, and Permian Resources. It gives a lot more confidence in earnings growth visibility.

For context, during 1H 2025, ~73% of WBI’s water-related revenue came from customers with credit ratings of BB- or higher. Given the long-duration nature of these contracts (and thereby cash flow for WBI), having low counterparty risk is paramount. Importantly, WBI is not overly reliant on any single customer. Its top five customers accounted for ~51% of water-related revenue in 1H 2025, with the largest contributing 18%.

Valuation

Between FY24 and FY26, I modeled the same EBITDA growth rate as FY24, 9%, which I think is reasonable given the existing asset base is already contracted, and there are inflation-linked mechanics to ensure pricing can go up. From FY27 onwards, EBITDA growth should see a step up as those new assets come online at higher pricing with better profitability. My idea here is that it should go above 9%, in the low-teens range (hence, I used 12%). Should this play out, I think WBI should trade easily at 14x forward EBITDA or more since peers like American States Water Co., American Water Works, York Water, and Global Water Resources all trade in the 13/14x forward EBITDA range with earnings growth of low teens.

Investment Risk

My primary concern here is execution risk. The growth story is great, but it is really tied to how well it can bring these large-scale projects online. Any significant construction delays, cost overruns, or a failure to ramp up volumes could impact returns. It would also weigh on the EBITDA growth outlook since these are the main drivers for growth acceleration.

Conclusion

WBI is an attractive long. It has a visible earnings growth profile with catalysts in place to drive growth acceleration in the coming years. Moreover, its access through LandBridge is a massive moat that should continue to give it pricing power in the foreseeable future. As large-scale projects come online, margin expansion and earnings visibility should improve meaningfully, and with all this in mind, I think WBI should trade at 14x forward EBITDA, at least in line with peers. All in all, I give a buy rating.

data.title: QDAY: Don't Sleep On These Overnight Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Growing up, my mother told me that nothing good ever happens after 2:00 AM. While that may have been good advice for a teenager, my mom didn’t know about the positive impact of afterhours trading hours on U.S. stock prices.

The Hamilton EnhancedTechnology DayMAX™ ETF (NEOE:QDAY:CA) is an innovative take on a call option ETF that seeks to capitalize on the market moments that happen outside of trading hours. One of the drawbacks of funds that employ call strategies, is that call options limit the capital gains potential of the underlying securities. QDAY is structured to mitigate this limitation by utilizing a zero-days-to-expiry (0DTE) call option strategy. This strategy allows for the generation on income through call options, while still allowing the fund to capture price movement that occurs outside of trading hours.

Fund Profile

Along with the Hamilton Enhanced Canadian Equity DayMAX ETF (CDAY:CA) and the Hamilton Enhanced U.S. Equity DayMAX ETF (SDAY:CA), QDAY is part of a suite of products Hamilton ETFs launched in July 2025. These DayMAX ETFs all feature 0DTE call options strategies to enhance income. According to Hamilton ETFs:

To generate income, the Portfolio Adviser actively manages an option strategy that will generally write at or slightly out of the money, primarily zero days to expiration (“0DTE”), or if deemed appropriate under specific market conditions, options with the shortest remaining maturity available, covered call options on an index (or indices) or ETF(s) with sufficient liquidity (as determined by the Portfolio Advisor), on up to 100% of the value of that position(s).

In addition to writing out of the money options, QDAY may also use covered calls or write put options on an index.

QDAY aims to aim to deliver frequent and premium levels of income by writing daily call options on a basket of underlying U.S. technology firms. This income potential is enhanced through the application of 25% leverage to the fund. QDAY holds two underlying ETFs:

XLK

Technology Select Sector SPDR

100.1%

QQQM

Invesco Nasdaq 100 ETF

24.5%

The nearly 125% portfolio allocation total reflects the 25% leverage the fund employs.

Launched in June 2025 with, QDAY has grown to attract $141.4M in assets. With an annual management fee of 0.85%, this fund is expensive, even by the standards of boutique ETFs like Hamilton. The high fees reflect the costly and complicated undertaking of actively managing an ultra-short-term options strategy.

After-hours Market Activity

While the vast majority of trading volume happens during daytime hours, there are many causes for stock price movements that occur after-hours.

Industry news, geopolitical events, earnings reports and other material disclosures are often outside of trading hours.

Harnessing "The Overnight Drift"

The idea of leveraging 0DTE options to capture the benefits of after-hours market fluctuations has been supported by research from the New York Federal Reserve Bank. In a 2020 staff report entitled "The Overnight Drift", by Nina Boyarchenko, Lars C. Larsen, and Paul Whelan, the authors argue that U.S. equity returns are substantially positive in after-hours trading, particularly during the opening hours of European markets. The authors note that these returns are economically impactful and highly statistically significant:

In fact, the largest positive returns are between 2:00 and 3:00 - the opening of European markets in U.S. Eastern Time terms - averaging 3.7% on an annualized basis (1.48 basis points per day). We dub these positive average returns the ‘overnight drift’ and argue that the pattern is most likely driven by the overnight resolution of order imbalances arising from the end of the preceding U.S. trading day.

To illustrate this "overnight drift" or the "night effect" we can see how overnight returns (represented by the blue line) outperform a standard continuously owned position (represented by the green line) in many, but not all time frames between 1993 and 2023.

While the night strategy has demonstrated positive returns over the past 20 years, Phil Mackintosh, Nasdaq Chief Economist, notes that this strategy has not worked consistently over the period. Mackintosh points out, that the night strategy significantly outperformed during the formation of the tech bubble, however the strategy has been less consistent since the early 2000’s.

Distributions

The investment objective of QDAY is to deliver “attractive income while providing exposure primarily to U.S. technology-focused equities.” This income-focused mandate informs the ETF’s strategy of frequent returns of capital in the form of income.

Since the fund’s launch in July 2025, QDAY has paid distributions on a semi-monthly basis. At launch these distributions were initiated at $0.20 per unit and have since risen to $0.2020 per unit. Extrapolating this first few months of activity suggests an annualized payout of $4.848 per unit. This distribution payout consistency and magnitude represents a 17.7% annualized yield at current levels.

Similar Funds

Before the advent of 0DTE ETFs, where this notion of night drift was leveraged by writing short term call options, a series of ETFs were created that attempted to buy the market at very close and sell again at every open. These pioneering NightShares ETFs closed in 2023 due to lackluster investor interest. Funds that have opted for a strategy of staying invested continuously while using options to take advantage of the night effect have been more successful.

Launched in July 2024, the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE) was the first ETF to harness the night effect through a 0DTE options strategy. This fund has amassed over $900M in assets in its first 15 months of operation. Offering a distribution date of a little over 30%, this fund has a weighty management fee of 0.97%.

YieldMax offers similarly structured funds including the YieldMax Nasdaq 100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTY), along with related funds covering the S&P 500 and the Russell 2000. Launched in 2024, this fund carries a gross expense ratio of 1.01%. To date, QDTY has only attracted $12M in assets.

Risk Analysis

Leverage

Charlie Munger, Warren Buffet's late partner, is said to have quipped that the only three things that could cause a smart person to go bankrupt were liquor, ladies and leverage. Leverage magnifies both gains and losses. While leverage has been an accelerant for funds in the recent period of strong market returns, the 25% of this fund that is owned through leverage could be very volatile in a market correction.

Options and Execution Risk

Even for professional traders using advanced tools, effectively and consistently using ultra short options is challenging. The funds employing these strategies are all relatively new, so there is no track record to speak of to demonstrate how these funds practically perform in different market conditions and durations. There is a risk that an instrument is temporarily mispriced, or that the cost of using options may exceed the returns.

In addition to the inherent market risk of an all-equity ETF, the use of options to generate income has the potential to limit the potential upside exposure of the underlying stocks. For equity holders with long time horizons, this is the most significant risk of owning QDAY. Considering the foregoing, it’s important to ensure that this fund aligns with an investor goals, as this ETF is primarily designed to generate income, rather than to provide exposure to long-term capital gains.

Investor Takeaways

QDAY combines daily call option writing and 1.25X leverage to enhance income and the frequency of distributions. Invested in a portfolio of U.S. technology stocks, QDAY retains exposure to equities while offering a large distribution. The use of actively managed, ultra-short-term, option strategies serves to mitigate the upside-limiting potential of options by ensuring that the fund is exposed to overnight price movements of underlying securities. While this ETF offers an innovative income strategy, the use of options may limit capital appreciation opportunities, therefore it may not be appropriate for long-term investors seeking growth. For investors seeking frequent income, the 17.7% annualized yield is attractive.

data.title: LyondellBasell: Dividend Sustainability Hinges On Downcycle, Not Management
data.description:
Intro

When an investor looks at LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (NYSE:LYB), the eye-catching dividend is certainly what drives attention. The 11+% yield it currently sports is not just considered high yield, but something in the "can't-be-true" category. As we know, there are no "free lunches," and the very next thought is probably “too good to be true” – in short, most investors probably think the dividend is not sustainable at this level.

While these may be the first thoughts, the next ones depend more on the individual and their previous experiences and knowledge. Some might immediately conclude that it is a value trap and that the dividend will soon be terminated or reduced. Others seek advice from trusted contacts who may have a better understanding, consult financial advisors, or look for appropriate analysis here on Seeking Alpha.

Since bets on the market, whether called an investment or a trade, are still bets on something unknown, there are as many different projections as there are people to ask. Predicting the future - even for management, seeing real-time data, is not an easy task. There are myriad moving parts to consider, while today’s economic environment is highly dynamic and remains in constant change.

While everyone has their own way of repacking the case, I will tap into my knowledge and experience to build my take ahead of the upcoming earnings expected on October 31st.

Rating

While LyondellBasell is a strong company with solid financials, having done this analysis, I still rate LYB as a Sell.

However, since the Sell rating is primarily due to the difficult downcycle the company is currently navigating, which, unfortunately, isn’t showing signs of improving in the near future, as I see it. If the cycle turns, I will definitely take a fresh look and re-evaluate the investment if I want to bet on the sector's upcycle.

Dividend Outlook

In my view, management will try to maintain the current dividend for as long as they can. The recent decision to raise it in May, in a way, also creates pressure to do so. It doesn’t look good from a management competence perspective to confidently raise the dividend only to cut it five or six months later. For me, the business outlook and current finances support cutting, although revised debt covenants give management time to prove otherwise.

What I’m Focusing On Regarding The Dividend's Sustainability

Cash Flow - Is there enough free cash flow to cover the dividends, and what is the cash flow outlook?

Net debt/EBITDA –The ratio gives good insight into the company’s debt sustainability. Financiers look at this carefully as well, and it's important from a credit rating perspective. Even if management wanted to proceed with the dividends, since cutting is definitely not a nice thing to do, there are credit agreements that simply will not allow the company to proceed.

The business outlook - This is highly connected with the first two points: it is highly likely that cash flow—and therefore the ability to pay dividends—will deteriorate if business volumes fall and the company's financials come under stress.

What the dividend history tells – How long has the dividend been paid, and how easily it has been terminated or changed in the past? A long and proud track record of consistent and growing dividends is often seen as a 'holy grail' for companies and their shareholders. These kinds of companies tend to fight until the very end before turning to a dividend cut.

Management's last comments about dividends – If the yield is at an exceptionally high level, it forces analysts to continuously question about its sustainability.

In my experience, these points largely outline the dividend outlook and safety outlook. While absolute certainty is rare in this world, gaining a solid understanding of the scale of likelihood is definitely achievable.

After the brief overview, I will re-evaluate Lyondell's dividend and business outlook in the chapters that follow.

Lyondell In brief

LyondellBasell is a multinational chemical company incorporated in the Netherlands. It’s one of the largest independent chemical manufacturers in the world, its US operations are headquartered in Houston, and its European headquarters are located in London and Rotterdam.

Lyondell describes its very beginning:

In 1953, LyondellBasell predecessor company scientist Professor Karl Ziegler discovers the first linear polyethylene (PE) chain. The following year, predecessor company scientist Giulio Natta polymerizes the first crystalline polypropylene.

LyondellBasell, as we know it today, was created in 2007 through the acquisition of Lyondell Chemical Company by Basell Polyolefins. The newly formed company was named LyondellBasell Industries.

The new company's beginning wasn't too smooth. As the subprime crisis pushed the world economy into crisis, LYB's U.S. operations filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in January 2009. The company successfully emerged from bankruptcy in April 2010. This new start got finalized when LYB was listed on the New York Stock Exchange on October 14, 2010.

Recent Developments

The last quarter's earnings were mixed. LYB reported $7.66 billion in revenue, down 11.8% YoY, but beating the consensus estimate by $149.53 million. However, the Earnings Per Share (EPS) of $0.62 missed analyst estimates by $0.16. Adjusted EBITDA fell sharply by 46.2% YoY, coming in at $715 million.

The following table provides an overview of LYB's largest segments and their dynamics:

As we can see, the most profitable segment, Olefins & Polyolefins – Americas, experienced the largest cut in both revenue and Adjusted EBITDA, if we exclude the Technology segment (which represents only 1.8% of total volumes).

The outlook provided by the company with the earnings release lacked any numeric metrics:

In the third quarter, the company expects North American integrated polyethylene margins to improve due to the completion of planned maintenance in April and increased prices supported by solid domestic demand and stronger export volumes. In Europe, steady seasonal demand and favorable feedstock costs are expected to continue. Ongoing capacity rationalizations across the region should help to balance regional supply and demand. Oxyfuels margins are expected to remain low for the remainder of the summer season. LYB continues to carefully evaluate potential risks and opportunities associated with evolving tariffs and global trade flows.

To align with global demand and the company's planned maintenance, LYB expects third quarter operating rates of 85% for North American olefins and polyolefins (O&P) assets, 75% for European O&P assets and 80% for Intermediates & Derivatives (I&D) assets.

Kimberly A. Foley, Executive Vice President of Global Olefins & Polyolefins, made probably the most optimistic statement during the earnings call:

You've seen export demand improve significantly over the last 2 months as some trade and tariff uncertainty has been resolved or paused.

Domestic demand is up year-on-year, quarter-on-quarter and month-on-month, hence, the support for the June increase. You see GDP numbers that are higher than we thought in China and in the U.S., though there may be some noise in the U.S. number. And most importantly, you also see global inventories very low, and we are in the middle of hurricane season. So for me, there are several signs of positive momentum. And if these tariff uncertainties get resolved in the next few weeks, there's absolutely potential for price increase in the third quarter.

The earnings presentation projected a relatively stable prognosis, with downward pressure anticipated only in the automotive segment:

Consensus Revenue and EPS Estimates by analysts as of October 13, 2025:

To summarize here, while management avoided any numerical predictions, they still communicated a quite optimistic view for the remainder of the year. Analysts, however, estimate the cycle will only be flattening out by the second quarter of 2026, and profitability will start to recover accordingly.

Downcycle

CEO Peter Vanacker shared his view on the current downcycle when asked about it during the recent earnings call:

this is the longest downturn in my 35 years in the industry. And with our strategic and also intentional portfolio management as well as our proactive cash management, we remain confident that we will navigate this cycle and emerge even stronger as a more sustainable and a more profitable leader for our industry.

As I understand from management comments and having delved through the available materials for this analysis, the current cycle is, in large part, not driven by demand issues—which remains relatively stable—but rather by significant oversupply. This oversupply, created mostly by new capacity coming online from China, has consequently driven prices down. While LyondellBasell has maintained its business volumes well, its profitability is currently under significant pressure.

A great summary about the current situation was provided by Sania Magdeburg, Vice President of Brenntag Specialties for the EMEA region, during the 59th annual EPCA meeting in Berlin (September 22–25, 2025):

We may face more challenges in the future. The industry is not panicking, but there is concern that this crisis may last longer than expected, and no one knows when a turnaround will occur. Although certain specialized application areas, such as nutritional chemicals and pharmaceutical chemicals, show some resilience, overall, the entire chemical industry is still collectively under market pressure.

I created the following tables to show the impact of the current downcycle on LyondellBasell’s different business segments.

In my view, the first table illustrates the real problem well. (Positive dynamics are marked with green, and negative with red.) Olefins and Polyolefins – Americas has been LYB’s most profitable segment, but it has recently seen its margins fall sharply.

As shown, selling volumes in kilotons actually rose quite nicely, but the cost of production is sharply higher, up roughly 40% year-over-year (YoY) in the recent quarters. Additionally, while U.S. ethylene prices rose strongly in the first quarter (Q1) YoY, they fell again rapidly on a sequential basis. Still, they remained almost stable YoY in 2Q, increasing by 3.66%, compared to the 26.45% growth seen in 1Q.

In summary, the situation looks poor in Olefins and Polyolefins – Americas segment when production costs rise by 40% while selling prices remain weak—clear evidence of downcycle dynamics. The inability to pass through sharply rising production costs when necessary is the key here, in my opinion.

The next table shows the EAI region dynamics, which are quite different from the U.S. Prices and volumes here are generally more stable. While the second quarter saw production costs fall, the average selling prices dropped accordingly.

Another key aspect is that the EAI region barely generates any profitability when products are produced domestically. This is a larger structural problem, as management has clearly communicated. Europe, in particular, is not competitive from a production standpoint, given current sector economics and the regulatory environment.

Since the European business has been a "problem child" for longer, its current performance is less evidence of the downcycle and more reflective of a persistent challenge to the profitability of LYB's overall European assets. The good news is that LYB has not lost any business here, as volumes are roughly the same, and Polypropylene even shows a nice +10% volume growth in the second quarter.

The next table shows that the Intermediates and Derivatives segment maintained quite stable volumes overall, but with some more rapid movements that mostly flatten out sequentially. For instance, styrene monomer volumes fell sharply in Q1, but had stabilized in Q2.

MTBE/ETBE saw a nice uptick of +23.58% in Q1, but cooled down the next quarter on a YoY comparison. Acetyls fell 14.41% in Q2, but had risen by a similar amount in the previous quarter. I think this can be taken as normal volatility. A telling "elephant in the room" of this cycle is the MTBE benchmark Market Margins, which saw strong deterioration in the first half of 2025.

All in all, these tables show, in my view, that demand is not the real problem, as volumes are relatively stable and have even risen in some segments. However, again, margins and profitability are currently under very significant pressure.

In my opinion, ChemOrbis summarizes the current situation well as of September 12:

Key PPH markets in the Middle East and Asia are retreating to levels not seen since the early pandemic era. Despite September being a typical peak-demand period, prolonged oversupply, subdued buying, and structurally weak global demand—particularly in automotive and durable goods sectors—have kept prices under pressure. China’s sluggish domestic consumption and rising self-sufficiency amid new capacity additions have further weighed on global sentiment, feeding into weakness across other regions.

In addition, neither Polypropylene nor Polyethylene shows any sign of bottoming as of October 13, 2025.

Polyethylene (CNY/T):

Polypropylene (CNY/T):

One can conclude here that the cycle is fully underway and its effects are clearly seen, with no sign of relief yet.

Valuation

In my opinion, in cases of relatively high indebtedness, it is always more appropriate to look at Enterprise Value (EV) multiples instead of the Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio, as the former also inherently takes the high debt level into account.

The following table shows that the LYB forward EV/Sales multiple is currently roughly 50% lower than the sector median and sits 11% below LYB's own historic multiple. The trailing EV/EBITDA metric is currently slightly lower than the sector median but slightly higher than the company's own historical ratio. The same forward-looking multiple is higher than both comparable ratios.

While these Enterprise Value comparisons offer some insight, they are still insufficient, in my view, to draw more precise conclusions, given the great unknown regarding how the current cycle will play out. Furthermore, the sector median includes a broad range of companies with their own very different cycle dynamics, meaning the presented LYB relative undervaluation may not provide an accurate view.

Given the underlying uncertainty, I view an EV/EBITDA of roughly 7x as a reasonable 'middle-of-the-road' valuation. This multiple is widely recognized as conservative for a large enterprise with limited or minimal growth prospects. My rough calculation assumes that both LYB's cash balance and EBITDA will see a slight decline in the coming quarters. To simplify and reflect a more directional modelling approach, I rounded the numbers to provide a simple "back-of-the-envelope" view. It doesn't make sense, in my view, to attempt precise modelling until there are clear signs of a cycle bottom, which would allow for more accurate modelling.

Share Price based on EV/EBITDA calculation, billions $

EBITDA

Since this is a rough calculation and does not pretend to a high level of accuracy, given the underlying circumstances, it's fair to conclude, in my view, that if the stock price falls into the low $30 price range, that would be the moment to take a more serious look at what has happened meantime and whether there are signs of better visibility.

Dividend Sustainability

As is sometimes the case, companies pay dividends even when ongoing profitability isn't high enough to support them. This is often achieved using previously accumulated liquidity reserves or even by taking on more debt, if the underlying financials allow it. In either case, the company expects better times ahead and aims to be shareholder-friendly.

As I wrote at the beginning of this analysis, I have specific criteria I use to assess whether a dividend can be considered sustainable and safe. I will now go through these points for the benefit of long-term dividend investors who don't want to time the market and instead choose to hold, believing the cycle will sooner or later turn around.

Cash Flow

The first half of 2025 doesn't look good, as the cash outflow of $1.25 billion is definitely a weak result:

The CFO downplayed the weak showing in the earnings call:

Cash used in operating activities of $228 million in the first six months of 2025 primarily reflected earnings adjusted for non-cash items, $384 million of tax payments which includes $235 million U.S. Federal corporate income tax payments deferred from 2024 into 2025 under Hurricane Beryl disaster relief, and a $433 million change in Accounts payable which was driven by the timing of payments.

The following table shows how the company utilized cash in its financing activities. Specifically, $201 million was used for share buybacks, and $878 million went to dividends. Additionally, $499 million in new debt was taken on.

To summarize here, the combined outflows for the 1H 2025: negative $1.25 billion in free cash flow, plus $1.079 billion paid out to shareholders (these two combined $2.329 billion), do not seem sustainable to me. The CFO provided his view on this situation when questioned by an analyst at the earnings call:

And I would also further point that our cash generation and cash conversion is usually much stronger in the second half of the year. So we are expecting -- if you recall, last year, the second half had a cash generation or cash conversion of 167% and just in Q4 alone, more than 280%.

So we are on target for strong cash conversion as well on the second half of the year. We expect positive cash flow from operations also in the second half of the year, and we are still on target to have our 80% cash conversion full year.

Should the situation play out as the CFO predicts, it would definitely soften the concerning results seen in the first half of the year. In addition, management presented the following actions being taken to improve the cash flow outlook:

The next slide presents the balance sheet's cash dynamics for Q2.

The $500 million in new debt ($499 million, to be exact, as per the SEC filing) was crucial, as the ending cash balance would otherwise have been approximately $1.2 billion. To put this in context, LYB needs approximately $445 million for the next dividend payment and an additional $490 million to cover maturing debt by year-end. If we assume cash flow doesn't improve in the next two quarters, these outflows would have nearly drained their cash reserves without the $500 million in new debt they took on during Q2.

Net debt/EBITDA

The very latest multiple and its underlying calculation have been taken directly from LYB’s SEC filing:

The ratio was quite close to the covenant previously agreed upon with LYB’s bankers. Had the financials deteriorated and the ratio climbed above, the company would have been restricted by lenders from paying a dividend. Consequently, as of September 10, 2025, the company signed an amendment to that agreement with its lenders to raise the covenant ratio as follows:

This means that LYB can proceed with dividends without restrictions from its financiers as long as the Net Debt/EBITDA ratio remains below these new multiples.

LYB's Debt Profile

In my view, it's important to highlight how the debt markets view the company's prospects: LYB's 10-year notes, recently issued at a yield of 6.15%, compare unfavorably to the 5.5% secured for the same duration last year. This suggests that debt markets are also pricing in higher risk. From LYB’s SEC filing:

In May 2025, we issued $500 million of 6.150% guaranteed notes due 2035. Net proceeds from the sale of the notes are used for general corporate purposes, including the repayment of our 2025 Notes later in the year. In February 2024, we issued $750 million of 5.5% guaranteed notes due 2034.

To add a bit more color to the overall debt profile: While the debt maturity profile is well-structured, the upcoming refinancing will be significantly more expensive. This is particularly true since the very next maturities are currently priced at really low yields (1.25% and 0.875%).

All in all, the debt profile is great, with nothing alarming in the near term. The key takeaway is that the next two refinancings will come with significantly higher price tags. My rough, rounded math shows that an additional 5% yield on $1 billion in debt will cost approximately $50 million more annually. While this is not overly significant for a company of LYB's scale, it still represents an additional ongoing cost.

Dividend History

It dates back to 2011, and it's important to note that since then, LYB has never cut the payout. Furthermore, the company has established a practice of regular annual dividend increases, which began in 2021. This commitment helps explain why management raised the dividend back in May of this year—marking the fifth consecutive annual increase—despite a clearly worsening business outlook at the time.

Management's Last Comments About Dividends

During the Q2 earnings call, CEO Peter Vanacker provided the following answer when asked by an analyst about his view on the dividend:

As part of our overall capital allocation strategy, we plan to provide returns to shareholders in the form of dividends and share repurchases. Barring any significant or unforeseen business challenges, mergers or acquisitions, over the long-term, we are targeting shareholder returns of 70% of free cash flow, defined as net cash provided by operating activities less capital expenditures. We intend to continue to declare and pay quarterly dividends, including a third quarter 2025 dividend of $1.37 per share. We have a goal of increasing the dividend over time, after giving consideration to our cash balances and expected results from operations. Our focus on funding our dividends while remaining committed to an investment grade balance sheet continues to be the foundation of our capital allocation strategy.

So in that context, we continue to recognize the importance of the dividend as a significant component of our returns to shareholders, and our actions continue to focus on securing our dividend while maintaining the benefits of our investment-grade rating. We're also not planning any further share buybacks over 2025 and 2026. We did some, as you know, in Q1. We did some in Q2. And our cash improvement plan is on track to improve our cash flow over the years 2025 and 2026, as set by at least, I mean, $1.1 billion.In addition to that, I mean, we believe the cycle will eventually rebound.

Risks

In my view, the company faces a significant feedstock risk: the AI boom is increasing energy demand, which has driven up natural gas prices. This is crucial, as natural gas is the main pillar of LYB's profitability and competitiveness.

I also see a political risk: if Chinese authorities view the sector as strategically important and choose to maintain or expand their market share, they could drive prices down over the long term, regardless of economic rationale.

Outside of that, I don't see LYB facing any other particular concentrated risk, as it is well-diversified across both markets and products. The primary challenge lies in the execution through the ongoing, serious downcycle and the length and depth of that cycle. Management must make prudent decisions to successfully navigate the company through these 'wild seas' to calmer waters.

The well-spoken macro and tariff risks are not specific to LYB but apply equally to any company in the sector.

The dividend is another key area that requires careful handling. While maintaining the dividend is certainly very important from a shareholder-friendly approach and long-standing traditional standpoint, however, from the perspective of the company's long-term financial sustainability, it might be better to step back on that front until the business cycle shows clear signs of improvement.

Takeaway

My view is that while LyodelBasell is a solid company, the cycle they are currently exposed not an easy one to get through. Management will likely try to keep the dividend, and as it said, “fight until the very end.” The latest SEC filing about covenants rise is great support for that thesis. Seems to me that if nothing extraordinary occurs and CFO projections about better cash flow hold water, investors likely receive at least the next quarter or two dividends at the current extraordinary high level.

However, the upcoming next quarter's cash flow statement and management’s comments have to be carefully listened to and will definitely give some additional clarity on what can be expected. If real improvements in the downcycle are not evident, management's ability to outmaneuver the weakening liquidity profile and leverage metrics without cutting the dividend will be limited in my view. Cost and CapEx cuts alone will not provide enough financial capacity to maintain the dividend at the current level.

data.title: Central Garden And Pet Traded Growth For Major Margin Gains
data.description:
Investors aren’t confident about Central Garden and Pet (NASDAQ:CENT), which makes pet food and lawn supplies, right now. The company’s stock price has fallen 17% in the past month. Its sales are falling, so it may look like it’s a declining business. But investors might not understand what the company is really doing and why it’s willing to give up potential sales.

For the third quarter of 2025, Central Garden and Pet posted $961 million in sales, a 3.6% decline. While this looks bad, it also reported that adjusted EPS rose 18% to $1.52. It also missed analysts’ estimates on revenue but beat their estimates on the bottom line. And performance like this is likely to continue for Central Garden and Pet. The company is currently implementing its Cost and Simplicity program, a long-term efficiency plan that’s designed to lower its costs.

Central Garden and Pet is willing to exit lower-margin markets and give up unprofitable business lines to raise its margins and get its costs down. As a result, it’s likely to post weaker revenue in the short term. But in the longer term, Central Garden and Pet has been able to expand by acquiring other pet food and garden products manufacturers, and a stronger balance sheet will allow the company to grow sales by making acquisitions in the future. There are several factors to consider here, so I’ll start by explaining why the company’s sales dropped and its profits rose this quarter.

Central Garden and Pet Is Exiting Less Profitable Markets

During the 3Q 2025 earnings call, Central Garden and Pet listed several markets that it’s exiting. It has decided to stop producing pet products in Britain and is selling its operations there. It’s also stopped producing lower-margin durable pet products and is focusing on consumables instead. And it stopped selling two garden product lines that were produced by a third-party manufacturer. The company also made cutbacks in its live plants business. These decisions caused Central Garden and Pet to incur additional shutdown costs for these operations as well.

Central Garden and Pet’s TTM operating margin is 8.1% according to Seeking Alpha, but its margin rose significantly this quarter. The company reported that during the third quarter of 2025, its operating margin was 14.1%, up 250 basis points from 11.6% in the third quarter of 2024. It appears that shutting down lower-margin business lines had a large impact on this quarter’s results.

The company is also shutting down two of its old distribution facilities and building a new distribution center in Salt Lake City. On the call, CEO Nicholas Lahanas said that the Salt Lake City distribution center would be operational in a month, which means it began operating in September during the company’s fourth fiscal quarter of 2025. The distribution center is designed to ship orders to DTC customers as well as the other stores that sell Central Garden and Pet’s products, such as pet stores and home improvement stores.

Organic Growth Is Unlikely in the Short Term for Central Pet and Garden

With that said, the company’s decision to shut down several operations and product lines is likely to result in negative revenue growth for the next few quarters. Central Garden and Pet doesn’t appear to have any organic revenue growth right now, and it’s unlikely to report any organic growth in the short term because of the overall market for pet products as well. I think some analysts might have bearish views on Central Garden and Pet because they think demand for pet products is falling, so I’ll explain why I consider the market stable right now.

In my recent article about the online store Chewy (CHWY), which is one of Central Garden and Pet’s customers, I went into more detail about the state of the pet products market. The Chewy CEO says that pet household formation is stable right now, so pet food sales should also be relatively stable. Pets still need to eat. And while private-label pet food is a potential threat to Central Garden and Pet’s market, the company has strong brands and Chewy’s private-label brands target high-income customers.

Central Garden and Pet also raised its guidance and called for non-GAAP EPS of $2.60 per share in the third-quarter earnings report. The company didn’t provide any 2025 guidance for revenue, which is unusual because most companies do that. But again, it reported -3.6% revenue growth in the third quarter and it posted -7.4% revenue growth in the second quarter. It’s likely that Central Garden and Pet expects negative revenue growth in the fourth quarter as well and doesn’t want to include that in its guidance. But I think it’s important to consider the bottom line for this company in particular because of the possibility of inorganic revenue growth in 2026.

Acquisitions Are a Possibility in 2026

Lahanas did discuss Central Garden and Pet’s acquisition strategy on the third-quarter 2025 earnings call. It sounds like management doesn’t see many great acquisition opportunities right now, but also feels like the business environment is improving. So the company plans to look for more acquisition opportunities next year and it’s looking for companies that manufacture consumables. For Central Garden and Pet, this category includes products such as grass seeds and bird seed as well as dog and cat food.

Central Garden and Pet’s last major acquisition was the purchase of the premium pet treat manufacturer TDBBS from the private equity firm Bregal Partners in 2023. TDBBS makes chewable treats such as bully sticks and jerky for dogs. On the call, management brought up the TDBBS acquisition and sounded confident about it. TDBBS has now been successfully integrated into Central Garden and Pet’s operations and Central Garden and Pet expects it to improve its future results as well.

It’s possible that Central Garden and Pet will attempt to buy another small pet food manufacturer that makes premium products such as health food for pets in 2026. This would give the company more access to rapidly growing market segments that have higher margins, and because premium pet food is consumable it would also gain repeat customers. But again, Central Garden faces competition from private-label products manufactured by its customers, such as Chewy, in this market as well.

Risks

Tariffs are a major risk factor for Central Garden and Pet. Like other manufacturers, it buys many of its products from China. On the call, the company explained that high inventory levels protected it from tariffs to some extent in 2025, but it now expects a $10 million impact from tariffs in the fourth quarter. It sounds like management plans to raise prices on its products and find suppliers outside of China to reduce the impact of tariffs in 2026.

The company-specific risk for Central Garden and Pet is that pet owners might decide to conserve their budgets by buying private-label food from pet stores. Gardeners might also decide to buy private-label products instead of brand-name ones. Central Garden and Pet does manufacture private-label products itself, so it won’t necessarily lose customers if this happens, but private-label products will have lower margins than its brand-name products.

Comparative Valuation

As a company with falling revenue, Central Garden and Pet is trading at low valuations. Its forward EV/sales ratio is 0.78, 46% lower than the median for household products makers. Its forward EV/EBITDA is 6.80, 35% below the household products median. It’s also trading at a price-to-book value ratio of 1.17.

A discount is justified because of Central Garden and Pet’s poor top-line results, but investors might not be fully considering the fact that this company also plans to continue making acquisitions and has the cash to do it.

Calculating Central Garden and Pet’s Value

Central Garden and Pet has raised its EPS guidance, and after calling for $2.20 in non-GAAP EPS in the second quarter, in the third quarter it said it now expects $2.60 in non-GAAP EPS. I’ll estimate that its revenue for 2025 will be $3.1 billion, which is a 3% decrease year-over-year. It has 64.9 million diluted shares outstanding so its net income would be $169 million for the full year. It also bought back 2 million shares of stock in 2025 because it didn’t find a suitable acquisition target.

Because Central Garden and Pet discontinued several businesses and is unlikely to grow organically next year, I’ll project a 3% fall in revenue to $3.0 billion for full-year 2026. The company's operating margin rose from 11.2% in the second quarter to 14.1% in the third quarter of 2025. It also said that its non-GAAP operating margin for the quarter was 14.5%. If I project that its full-year operating margin could be 12.5% next year, and subtract 50 basis points for tariff impact, at a 12% margin the company would post $360 million in operating profit. If I subtract $30 million in net interest expenses because the company appears to be paying down debt and assume a 25% tax rate, that’s $248 million in net income.

At a 2026 forward P/E of 12, Central Garden and Pet would have a market cap of $2.98 billion. If I assume that Central Garden and Pet doesn’t find a company to acquire, it could buy back 2 million shares in 2026, which would reduce its share count to 62.9 million. Dividing a market cap of $2.98 billion by 62.9 million shares results in a target price of $47 per share. Central Garden and Pet’s closing price was $30 on October 15, so my price target is 57% higher than the current price.

Wall Street analysts currently have a target price of $42 for Central Garden and Pet, which is 43% higher than the current price. SA analysts currently rate this stock a hold, Wall Street analysts rate it a buy, and the SA quant score says it’s a hold.

Conclusion

This is going to be a contrarian call, but I’d say that Central Garden and Pet is a strong buy. It appears that investors are only looking at its top-line results and not its bottom-line performance, and I consider the recent selloff unjustified because of this factor. By shutting down lower-margin product lines and exiting unprofitable markets, Central Garden and Pet has made major improvements to its margins. And even though its organic revenue is expected to fall, this company has the cash to acquire another pet food manufacturer and can grow sales that way as well.

data.title: Beyond Meat: Convertible Note Exchange Buys It Some Additional Time
data.description:
Beyond Meat (NASDAQ:BYND) announced that approximately 97% of its $1.15 billion in convertible notes due March 2027 were tendered by the early tender deadline for its exchange offer.

This should buy Beyond Meat some additional time to try to rescue its business, although its situation is still fairly dire. It has fully drawn its $100 million Delayed Draw Term Loan now, and I currently project it to run out of cash by early 2027 (without further fundraising) in my base case scenario.

I expect Beyond Meat to do a reverse split in the future as well as issue more shares to help fund its business. It will seek approval for a future reverse split at a special meeting along with an increase in authorized shares to 3 billion.

Although Beyond Meat is reducing its net debt by over $800 million with this exchange offer, I still believe that the intrinsic value of its common stock is zero.

Beyond Meat's outstanding debt may reach around $475 million by early 2030 due to the PIK interest on its new notes and its term loan.

Exchange Offer

Beyond Meat is attempting to exchange up to all $1.15 billion of its 0% Convertible Senior Notes due 2027 for up to $202.5 million in new 7.0% Convertible Senior Secured Second Lien PIK Toggle Notes due 2030 and up to 326.2 million shares of its common stock.

Each $1,000 in 0% Convertible Senior Notes due 2027 can be exchanged (if tendered by the early tender deadline) for $176.087 in 7.0% Convertible Second Lien PIK Toggle Notes due 2030 and 283.6438 shares of Common Stock.

Around 97% of the Convertible Senior Notes due 2027 were tendered by the early tender deadline.

Convertible note holders are ending up owning approximately 81% of Beyond Meat's common stock.

The conversion rate for the new convertible notes will be determined later, but could easily result in another 100 million (or more) shares if those new notes are converted later. That could result in convertible note holders ending up with a total of 85+% of Beyond Meat's stock if the new notes are converted in full.

Effect On Beyond Meat

As it stands, $1.115 billion in Convertible Senior Notes due 2027 were tendered by the early tender deadline. If no more notes are tendered, then Beyond Meat will end up issuing approximately $209 million in new 7.0% Convertible Second Lien PIK Toggle Notes due 2030 (including the $12.5 million new notes going to supporting noteholders) and approximately 316 million shares.

The new convertible notes have a 9.5% interest rate if paid-in-kind. I expect Beyond Meat to choose that option due to its need to preserve cash.

Thus by March 2027, Beyond Meat is projected to have $390 million in outstanding debt.

March 2027 Projected Debt $ Million
Delayed Draw Term Loan $119
New Convertible Notes Due 2030 $236
Old Convertible Notes Due 2027 $35

It will also have $35 million in old convertible notes maturing in March 2027 (if no more are tendered).

Projected Cash Position

I currently project Beyond Meat to have around $5 million in unrestricted cash remaining in March 2027, before any proceeds from its ATM offering. This is due to Beyond Meat's continued cash burn as well as the costs associated with this exchange offer.

Beyond Meat had $103 million in unrestricted cash at the end of Q2 2025. It drew another $60 million from its Delayed Draw Term Loan subsequent to the end of the quarter. Beyond Meat also projected $39 million in fees and expenses related to the exchange offer, of which $5 million had been paid by the end of Q2 2025.

Thus proforma for those items, Beyond Meat would have $129 million in unrestricted cash.

Beyond Meat reported negative $22 million adjusted EBITDA in Q2 2025 (and Q2 is typically its seasonally strongest quarter). I am projecting negative $40 million in adjusted EBITDA and $5 million in capex for the second half of 2025, which would bring its unrestricted cash down to $84 million at the end of 2025.

An improvement to negative $60 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2026 (compared to negative $104 million in 2025) along with $10 million in capex next year would leave it with $14 million in unrestricted cash at the end of 2026.

Beyond Meat may then have another $9 million in cash burn in the first 2.5 months of 2027, leaving it with $5 million in unrestricted cash when its remaining old convertible notes mature on March 15, 2027. Since there are $35 million in remaining old convertible notes, Beyond Meat will run out of unrestricted cash at this point (without additional fundraising) trying to repay those notes.

If Beyond Meat raises another $100 million through equity offerings, it would be projected to have $70 million in unrestricted cash after paying off the old convertible notes.

Beyond Meat also mentioned that the arbitration with a former co-manufacturer was going well (with the arbitrator finding that Beyond Meat had a valid basis to terminate the agreement). This could be a potential source of additional funds (potential amount unknown) for Beyond Meat.

Even with some potential money coming that way, it still seems likely that Beyond Meat will need to raise more money through its ATM offering to help repay its remaining old convertible notes due March 2027 as well as fund continued operations after that.

Notes On Valuation

I had previously mentioned that I believed that Beyond Meat's common stock had an intrinsic value of zero due to its massive debt burden. The main thing keeping Beyond Meat's stock afloat at $2+ per share was its large short interest and the uncertainty about exactly when (it could have been next week to past next year) it would restructure its debt.

The exchange offer is reducing Beyond Meat's net debt by over $800 million, but I still believe Beyond Meat's common stock has zero intrinsic value. The exchange offer gives it additional time to work on a turnaround, but it doesn't anything for Beyond Meat's substantially negative free cash flow.

Beyond Meat's debt is also increasing due to the projected PIK interest. The Delayed Draw Term Loan may have $166 million outstanding by its February 2030 maturity date. At that time, there may also be around $308 million of its new convertible notes outstanding.

Without substantial improvement in its business, I'd have a hard time valuing Beyond Meat at more than $200 million.

As well, Beyond Meat's outstanding shares are quintupling, even before additional potential dilution to fund operations. Beyond Meat is seeking approval for a future reverse split as well as an increase in authorized shares to 3 billion (from 500 million).

Conclusion

Beyond Meat's note exchange is reducing its net debt by over $800 million and relieves it of needing to repay a massive amount of debt in March 2027.

However, Beyond Meat still has major challenges. Even with moderate improvements in its business, I project it to run out of unrestricted cash by March 2027 without additional fundraising. It still has $35 million in old convertible notes outstanding that mature in March 2027 and I currently projected it to have $5 million in unrestricted cash in March 2027 before paying off those notes.

Beyond Meat's share count is quintupling from the exchange offer and it is seeking to increase its authorized shares to 3 billion to give it flexibility to raise more funds.

With the PIK interest adding to its outstanding debt, I believe it will be hard for Beyond Meat's common equity to have above zero intrinsic value. Beyond Meat's remaining old convertible notes show that the market believes Beyond Meat's chance of longer-term survival is quite questionable too. These notes have recently been trading at 31 to 43 cents on the dollar after the early tender results were announced, indicating there is still considerable uncertainty about whether Beyond Meat will be able to repay that modest $35 million note maturity in March 2027.
